meta.title: What Market-Based Instrument Are You?
meta.subtitle: A BuzzFeed-style vibe check for the neoliberal valuation of nature.
meta.intro: Market-based instruments promise to save the planet by pricing it. Find out which one you are — the all-rounder nobody can define, the techno-fix bachelor, or the shiny offset that looks great until someone checks the receipts. Answers are weighted; ecosystem, infrastructure, and happiest-place questions do the heavy lifting, the rest are vibes.

nbs.name: Nature-based Solution
nbs.subtitle: The all-rounder. Please do not ask for a definition.
nbs.description: You are the policy world's favorite umbrella term. You do everything — flood protection, carbon, biodiversity, urban cooling, community wellbeing — which is mostly great, except nobody can agree on what you actually are. You thrive in funding proposals and IUCN side events. You are beloved, slightly vague, and doing your best.
whyItFits: You promise co-benefits without specifying whose.
You are cited in every policy paper and defined in none.
You are what people mean when they say 'win-win.'

nature_as_infrastructure.name: Nature as Infrastructure
nature_as_infrastructure.subtitle: NbS's hipper, slightly unhinged younger sibling.
nature_as_infrastructure.badge: The Engineer in a Field Jacket
nature_as_infrastructure.description: You took one look at NbS and said 'but what if we quantified it harder?' You reframe ecosystems as service-delivery assets on municipal balance sheets. You are beloved by World Bank reports and confusing at dinner parties. Mangroves are your levees. Wetlands are your stormwater utility. Nature works for you, literally.
whyItFits: You turn ecosystems into line items.
You speak fluent procurement and fluent mycelium.
You are mostly a pitch deck in a trench coat.

carbon_offset_biodiversity.name: Carbon Offset with Biodiversity Co-benefit
carbon_offset_biodiversity.subtitle: They say you can do it all. You cannot.
carbon_offset_biodiversity.description: Everyone wants to play with you. You promise carbon neutrality AND charismatic megafauna in one tidy credit. You are featured in airline ads and sustainability reports. The problem is if anyone looks too closely they notice the additionality is vibes and the biodiversity metric is a bird list from 2014. You remain very popular.
whyItFits: You bundle two goods to distract from one problem.
You photograph extremely well.
Verification is a personal journey, not a requirement.

ccs.name: Carbon Capture & Storage
ccs.subtitle: The techno-fix bachelor who refuses to go to therapy.
ccs.description: You are the engineering-school golden child who thinks we can drill our way out of this. You have a PowerPoint and three pilot projects, two of which are delayed. Fossil fuel companies love you. You avoid the words 'demand reduction' the way a cat avoids water. You will, you insist, scale any day now.
whyItFits: You prefer a technical fix to a political one.
Your pilot phase has a pilot phase.
You are somehow both the future and 2007.

pes.name: Payments for Ecosystem Services
pes.subtitle: Paying nature to show up to work.
pes.badge: The HR Department of the Biosphere
pes.description: You look at a watershed and see a contractor. You believe rural communities will steward landscapes if you wire them the right monthly transfer. Sometimes this works. Sometimes it dispossesses people from commons they already managed for free. You mean well, mostly, and you have very good spreadsheets.
whyItFits: You convert stewardship into a service contract.
You love a pilot in Costa Rica.
You ask 'what would motivate a landowner?' instead of 'who owns the land?'

biodiversity_credits.name: Biodiversity Credits
biodiversity_credits.subtitle: Carbon offsets' ambitious cousin, already under scrutiny.
biodiversity_credits.description: You are new, you are glossy, and the ink on your methodology is still wet. Investors are excited. Indigenous and local communities are asking sharp questions you do not want to answer. You claim to make biodiversity 'investable,' which is either the future of conservation or a category error, depending on who you ask at the conference.
whyItFits: You treat habitat as a tradable unit.
Your methodology is 'emerging' (unfinished).
You were launched at COP with a sizzle reel.

redd_plus.name: REDD+
redd_plus.subtitle: The aging celebrity with a complicated colonial past.
redd_plus.description: You have been around since Bali 2007. You were going to end deforestation. Instead you generated academic careers, a vast compliance industry, and several lawsuits about overlapping land claims. You are still here, reformulating yourself for the voluntary market. You carry the entire history of forest carbon in one tired acronym.
whyItFits: You have a longer publication record than most professors.
You are constantly being 'reformed.'
You remember when 'permanence' was still a debate, not a disclaimer.

questions.prompt: Which ecosystem do you identify with?

answers.text: Wetland — chaotic, underrated, filtering everyone's mess.
Forest — long-lived, charismatic, extremely monetizable.
Desert & semi-arid — austere, overlooked by funders, secretly essential.
Mangroves — the policy world's current favorite.
Grasslands — quietly storing carbon while everyone ignores you.
Ocean — vast, unmeasured, absolutely about to be financialized.

questions.prompt: Which infrastructure project is most appealing?

answers.text: A big bridge — iconic, expensive, photo-ready.
A road — practical, extractive-adjacent, always getting widened.
A railway — efficient, collective, slightly 19th century.
A house — individual, domestic, quietly ideological.
A canal — slow, beautiful, deeply Dutch-engineered energy.
A harbour — global trade, colonial residue, container chic.

questions.prompt: Where would you feel the happiest?

answers.text: City — dense, legible, full of stakeholders.
Beach — leisure economy, rising sea, sunscreen diplomacy.
Mountain — lofty, spiritual, excellent for carbon narratives.
Wetland — squelchy, underestimated, home.
A palace in the sky — aspirational, untethered, Davos-coded.
A hole in the ground, Dune-style — ascetic, sand-pilled.

questions.prompt: What service that nature provides to you would you rank the highest?

answers.text: Recreation — hiking, swimming, being outside as content.
Air & water filtration — the quiet load-bearing service.
Iconic species — whales, tigers, pandas, donor appeal.
Good Vibes! — spiritual, unquantifiable, deeply real.
Pearls — extractive, glossy, mildly feudal.
Existence of biodiversity — intrinsic, unpriced, under threat.

questions.prompt: What environmental catastrophe are you most afraid of?

answers.text: Sea level rise swallowing the coast (and my property value).
Total deforestation and the loss of the last old-growth.
The sixth mass extinction, but make it spreadsheet-legible.
Runaway warming and tipping points no model can catch.
The enclosure and financialization of what was once common.
Taps running dry while bottled water futures boom.

questions.prompt: How would you prefer to be killed by nature?

answers.text: Sink in a bog and be preserved forever, Iron Age icon.
A big wave of water, 2012-style. Dramatic. Cinematic.
An avalanche while you ski a carbon-offset slope.
Swallowed by the earth via a sinkhole on a CCS pipeline.
A small, unbothered, extremely venomous creature.
An ancient tree falls on you. Rude, but narratively satisfying.
Moss green — foundational, quietly competent.
Concrete grey — structural, urban, honest.
Electric teal — pitch-deck chic, conference-lanyard friendly.
Dusty gold — heritage, donor report, 'legacy' energy.
Terracotta — earthy, co-op, subsidy-eligible.
Oil-slick iridescent — pretty, ominous, unaccountable.
Fieldwork chic — muddy boots, linen shirt, one expensive hat.
Consultant casual — patagonia vest, lanyard, urgent laptop.
High-vis and steel toes — you are here to deploy something.
Cottagecore — apron, sourdough, small and quiet stewardship.
COP delegate power-dress — blazer, silk scarf, passport in hand.
Earthy academic — hemp, glasses, unread Latour in tote bag.
Bicycle — honest, cheap, politically virtuous.
Train — collective, infrastructural, quietly superior.
Electric SUV — large, tax-credited, morally confusing.
Private jet, offset of course.
A horse — pre-industrial, aristocratic, low-methane-ish.
Sailing yacht — blue-economy-coded, very ocean-forum.
